steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses joined by a semicolon: Nakalembe doesn't just compile the information, though (clause 1) and she also shares her findings with African farmers... (clause 2). The word though is a transitional interrupter that belongs to clause 1 — it is set off by a comma and placed at the end of that clause before the semicolon.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule and eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
Two independent clauses require a semicolon between them. The correct pattern is information, though; she also... — a comma before though (setting it off within clause 1) and a semicolon after it (separating the two independent clauses). That is Choice A — ‘information, though;’.

An incorrect response places a comma after though instead of a semicolon, producing a comma splice — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. An incorrect response puts a semicolon before though and moves it to the start of clause 2 (though she also shares...), making clause 2 a dependent clause; a semicolon cannot precede a dependent clause. Choice D (‘information though,’) omits punctuation between the clauses entirely, producing a run-on sentence.

The answer is A.
